summary: Reviewed the remediation specification rather than an implementation diff; the pack contains no changed files and the spec is truncated after the Date-TBD section, so several architectural checks cannot be completed from the supplied evidence. The included sections generally identify the right integration surfaces, but a few proposed fixes risk moving business rules into presentation/type layers or leaving system components with divergent state contracts.
appears_sound: The spec correctly groups fixes by integration area and explicitly orders foundational status/data-model fixes before downstream SMS, contract, and revalidation consumers in `tasks/private-bookings-review/remediation-spec.md:17`.

The D4 plan centralizes status transition validation so both `updateBooking()` and `updateBookingStatus()` share one transition contract instead of maintaining separate rules in `tasks/private-bookings-review/remediation-spec.md:75`.

The D1 plan preserves the existing non-null `event_date` downstream contract while changing lifecycle ownership to `hold_expiry = null` for TBD bookings in `tasks/private-bookings-review/remediation-spec.md:135`.

findings.description: The spec says to extract `ALLOWED_TRANSITIONS` into `types.ts`, which likely turns a type module into a business-rule owner. That creates a coupling path where UI and domain mutation code both import operational workflow rules from a file whose responsibility should be contracts, not policy.
findings.evidence: D4 explicitly instructs: `Remove the duplicate ALLOWED_TRANSITIONS ... Extract to a shared constant in types.ts and import in both functions` at `tasks/private-bookings-review/remediation-spec.md:75`.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: If this repo already stores domain constants beside private-booking types, this may match an established local convention.
findings.what_would_confirm: Check the existing `types.ts` contents and nearby private-booking modules to see whether domain constants live there or in a dedicated domain/config module.

findings.description: The Date-TBD fix keeps storing a fake `event_date` while making `hold_expiry` and balance due date nullable, but the spec does not define a single canonical state contract for all consumers. This risks downstream features continuing to treat `event_date` as real and producing inconsistent calendar, query, payment, SMS, or contract behavior.
findings.evidence: D1 says `Still store a date ... but set hold_expiry to null` while only explicitly updating create logic and creation SMS in `tasks/private-bookings-review/remediation-spec.md:126` and `tasks/private-bookings-review/remediation-spec.md:153`.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The omitted, truncated portion of the spec may enumerate every downstream consumer that must switch to the TBD flag instead of trusting `event_date`.
findings.what_would_confirm: Review the full remediation spec or implementation plan for all readers of `event_date`, especially queries, calendar views, contract generation, scheduled SMS, and payment due-date logic.

findings.description: The edit-page redirect for completed or cancelled bookings is specified only at the UI route layer, while the server action still permits status-only changes for cancelled bookings. The spec depends on the detail page being the sole owner of lifecycle transitions, but does not state that ownership boundary or ensure other callers cannot accidentally use `updateBooking()` for status transitions.
findings.evidence: D12 says the edit route should redirect for completed or cancelled bookings, but also says `Status transitions on cancelled bookings ... still work via the detail page's status action` in `tasks/private-bookings-review/remediation-spec.md:78` and `tasks/private-bookings-review/remediation-spec.md:101`.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The implementation may already have a clear separate `updateBookingStatus()` action used by the detail page only.
findings.what_would_confirm: Confirm all status-changing UI and API paths call `updateBookingStatus()` rather than sending status through the broad `updateBooking()` payload.

findings.description: The deposit guard is specified inside `finalizeDepositPaymentWithClient()`, but the pack does not show whether all manual, PayPal, webhook, and reconciliation flows pass through that function. If any deposit-recording path bypasses it, status immutability remains split across multiple ownership points.
findings.evidence: D13 changes only `finalizeDepositPaymentWithClient()` while acceptance criteria cover both manual and PayPal deposit capture in `tasks/private-bookings-review/remediation-spec.md:116` and `tasks/private-bookings-review/remediation-spec.md:121`.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The named function may already be the shared gateway for every deposit capture path.
findings.what_would_confirm: Trace manual deposit recording, PayPal capture, PayPal webhook, and any admin reconciliation paths to verify they all call `finalizeDepositPaymentWithClient()` before persisting payment state.

findings.description: Most of the remediation plan cannot be reviewed for architectural fit because the pack has no changed files, no related files, and the spec is truncated before groups C-L. That prevents validating the largest integration risks called out by the user, including SMS cancellation, contract legal text, permission model changes, migration behavior, and transaction safety.
findings.evidence: The pack states `Changed Files (none detected)`, `Changed File Contents (no files to include)`, `Related Files (no related files found)`, and `[spec truncated at line 200]`.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: This may be intentional for a spec-only review, with later reviewers expected to inspect the full implementation pack.
findings.what_would_confirm: Provide the full remediation spec and the relevant source files or implementation diff for the private-bookings modules, cron routes, contract template, queries, and migration.

empty_categories: No concrete circular dependency or client/server import violation can be confirmed from the supplied pack.
No concrete schema or migration incompatibility can be confirmed because the migration text and database model are not included.
No stale-cache architecture finding can be confirmed because the revalidation section is outside the visible spec excerpt.
No raw data-access boundary violation can be confirmed because no implementation diff or changed file contents are present.
